history: 55-year-old male with hypertension (controlled on medication for 10 years) and dyslipidemia (on medication for 5 years)
presentation: Presented with 2 episodes of vomiting for 1 day
diagnostics: No significant abnormality on systemic examination
Basic blood tests (CBC with differentials, comprehensive metabolic panel) within physiological limits

Non-contrast CT brain: hyperdense lesion anterior to brainstem within prepontine cistern, compression of 4th ventricle, dilated bilateral lateral ventricles with periventricular seepage suggestive of hydrocephalus

CT cerebral angiography: partially thrombosed fusiform aneurysm of left vertebral artery (2.8 cm diameter, 3.2 cm length), non-enhancing hypodense area in peripheral portion representing thrombosis, contrast opacification in central portion representing residual lumen
Dolichoectasia of V4 segment of left vertebral and basilar artery with irregular areas of dilatation
Ectatic left vertebral artery crossing midline to right side
Absent A1 segment of left ACA with bilateral ACA arising from A1 segment of right ACA
Ectasia of terminal segment of right internal carotid, proximal M1 segment of right MCA, and proximal A1 segment of right ACA
Other places for thrombus ruled out with echocardiogram and CT chest angiography
No provoked cause of thrombus from history
Diagnosis: obstructive hydrocephalus due to partially thrombosed fusiform aneurysm of left vertebral artery with ectasia of anterior and posterior cerebral circulation
management: Symptomatic treatment with analgesics for headache and antiemetics for vomiting
Counseling for anticoagulation (apixaban 10 mg for a week followed by 5 mg twice daily), started after four days
Antihypertensive medicines optimized for blood pressure control
Ongoing discussions about further management at each follow-up
outcome: Partial relief of headache at 1 month follow-up
Doing better and symptoms partially in control at 3 months follow-up
